summary: The verifier failure was narrow: delegation use/accounting state was effectively keyed by a derived id that reused the same source/target/secret tuple. A tiny emitter-side uniqueness fix was enough.
ergonomics: The delegated secret runtime is concentrated in `src/Clasp/Emit/JavaScript.hs`, so this was a local patch instead of a cross-pipeline change.
The existing scenario test in `test/Main.hs` already exercised real handoff flows, which made it easy to extend with a concrete regression instead of inventing a new fixture.
follow_ups: If delegation state needs to survive reloads, add an explicit persisted issuance nonce source instead of the current in-memory counter.
Add a first-class regression for bounded-use accounting itself once that surface is exposed clearly in public tests, not just uniqueness of issuance ids.
warnings: The delegation `id` looks semantic because it embeds source/target/secret names, but hidden state may depend on it being issuance-unique; do not derive it only from endpoint tuples.

Searches for delegation logic in the emitter are noisy because broader secret runtime helpers sit nearby; start around the `clasp-secret-delegation` literals and `handoff` regression before widening scope.
tests_run: nix develop -c bash -lc 'cabal test --test-options='"'"'--pattern="delegated secret handoffs pass handles across agent tool and workflow boundaries without raw values"'"'"''
residual_risks: Delegation ids are now unique via a module-local monotonic counter, so they are not stable across fresh process/module reloads. That is fine for runtime-local use accounting, but any future persistence layer should not assume cross-process determinism.
